title: Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
subtitle: The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.
caption:
Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, 2023

The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

The choropleth map displays the estimated rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition per 100,000 children under the age of 5 across the globe in 2023. A continuous color scale ranging from light beige to deep maroon indicates the magnitude of mortality, with the legend marking thresholds at 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 deaths. The highest values appear concentrated in a band across sub-Saharan Africa, where countries including Chad, the Central African Republic, Somalia, and Mali fall into the darkest maroon band representing rates exceeding 1,000 deaths per 100,000 children. Surrounding nations in West and East Africa, such as Nigeria, Ethiopia, and Sudan, show elevated rates in the 200 to 1,000 range, depicted in medium to dark red tones. In South Asia, Afghanistan and Pakistan display rates in the 100 to 500 band, while India and Bangladesh appear in lighter orange shades corresponding to the 50 to 200 range. Most of Latin America, including Brazil and Mexico, along with parts of Southeast Asia like Indonesia, fall within the 20 to 100 band. The lowest rates, shown in pale beige, cover nearly all of North America, Europe, Australia, and East Asian nations like China and Japan, where values sit below 20 deaths per 100,000 children. Greenland and a few small island states are marked as having no data. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high mortality rates in specific regions of Africa and parts of Asia, while low rates dominate the Global North and Oceania.
